questions.questionText: The most common primary malignant bone tumor is:
questions.optionB: Osteosarcoma (peaks in 2nd decade, metaphysis around knee — distal femur most common site, associated with Paget's disease in elderly)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Osteosarcoma = most common primary malignant bone tumor (excluding myeloma). Why correct: Age: bimodal (10-20 years primary; >60 years secondary to Paget's). Location: metaphysis around knee (distal femur #1, proximal tibia #2). Radiology: sunburst periosteal reaction, Codman triangle, mixed lytic-sclerotic. Why A wrong: Ewing = 2nd most common, diaphysis, onion-peel, age 5-15. Why C wrong: Chondrosarcoma = older adults (>40), pelvis/proximal femur. Why D wrong: GCT is benign aggressive (not malignant) — epiphyseal, age 20-40. Clinical relevance: Elevated ALP (osteoblastic). Treatment: neoadjuvant chemotherapy + limb-salvage surgery + adjuvant chemotherapy. Exam trap: If myeloma included, it's the most common malignant bone neoplasm overall. If excluded (as it's a marrow tumor), osteosarcoma is #1. Memory aid: 'Osteosarcoma = #1 malignant in bone, 2nd decade, metaphyseal, around knee.' Reference: Maheshwari – Bone Tumors.
questions.conceptTag: Osteosarcoma most common malignant bone metaphysis knee
